title: Resident Training Module: MEGATite 160mg Tablet (Megestrol Acetate)

items.title: Module Overview
items.content:
# Resident Training Module: MEGATite 160mg Tablet (Megestrol Acetate 160mg)

---

## 📋 MODULE OVERVIEW

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Brand Name** | MEGATite 160mg Tablet |
| **Generic Name** | Megestrol Acetate |
| **Strength** | 160mg per tablet |
| **Pack Size** | 10 Tablets |
| **Drug Class** | Synthetic Progestational Agent (Progestin) |
| **Schedule** | Prescription Only (Rx) |
| **Route** | Oral |

---

items.title: MECHANISM OF ACTION
items.content:
### Primary Mechanism
Megestrol acetate is a **synthetic derivative of progesterone** with the following pharmacological actions:

1. **Antineoplastic Activity:**
   - Suppresses luteinizing hormone (LH) release from the anterior pituitary
   - Reduces estrogen levels through hormonal modulation
   - Direct cytotoxic effect on hormone-sensitive tumor cells
   - Inhibits estrogen receptor expression in tumor tissue
   - Blocks aromatase enzyme activity (partial)

2. **Appetite Stimulation / Anabolic Activity:**
   - Stimulates appetite through modulation of **neuropeptide Y** in the hypothalamus
   - Downregulates pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α)
   - Increases body weight primarily through **fat accumulation**
   - Modulates serotonin pathways involved in satiety

3. **Progestational Activity:**
   - Binds to progesterone receptors
   - Induces secretory transformation of the endometrium
   - Suppresses gonadotropin release (LH and FSH)

---

items.title: ALL INDICATIONS
items.content:
### A. FDA-APPROVED / PRIMARY INDICATIONS

---

### **1. Breast Cancer (Advanced/Metastatic)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Indication** | Palliative treatment of advanced (recurrent, inoperable, or metastatic) breast cancer |
| **Patient Population** | Postmenopausal women |
| **Line of Therapy** | Second-line or third-line hormonal therapy |
| **Receptor Status** | Hormone receptor-positive (ER+/PR+) |
| **Dosage** | 160mg (1 tablet) QID (total 640mg/day) or 160mg OD–QID based on protocol |
| **Duration** | Minimum 2 months to assess response; continue if responding |
| **Response Rate** | 20–30% objective response rate |

**Clinical Pearls:**
- Used after failure of tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors
- Better response in PR-positive tumors
- Monitor for thromboembolic events
- Median time to response: 6–8 weeks

---

### **2. Endometrial Cancer (Advanced/Recurrent)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Indication** | Palliative treatment of advanced or recurrent endometrial carcinoma |
| **Line of Therapy** | Second-line after surgery/radiation or when not amenable to surgery |
| **Dosage** | 40–320mg/day in divided doses (commonly 160mg BID–QID) |
| **Duration** | Continue for minimum 2 months; reassess response |
| **Response Rate** | 15–25% in well-differentiated tumors |
| **Best Responders** | Well-differentiated, hormone receptor-positive tumors |

**Clinical Pearls:**
- Higher response rates in Grade 1 (well-differentiated) tumors
- Can be used in patients unfit for chemotherapy
- PR expression is the strongest predictor of response
- Consider in fertility-sparing management of early endometrial cancer (off-label, complex atypical hyperplasia)

---

### **3. Cancer-Associated Anorexia-Cachexia Syndrome (CACS)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Indication** | Anorexia, cachexia, or unexplained significant weight loss in cancer patients |
| **Dosage** | 160mg–800mg/day (commonly start 160mg BID–TID) |
| **Optimal Dose** | 400–800mg/day for appetite stimulation |
| **Response** | Appetite improvement in 60–80% of patients |
| **Weight Gain** | Average 2–5 kg over 8–12 weeks |
| **Duration** | Weeks to months; reassess periodically |

**Clinical Pearls:**
- Weight gain is primarily **fat, not lean muscle mass**
- Combines well with nutritional counseling
- Does NOT improve survival but improves quality of life
- Begin early in cachexia trajectory for best results

---

### **4. HIV/AIDS-Related Wasting Syndrome**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Indication** | Anorexia, cachexia, or significant unexplained weight loss (>10% baseline) in HIV/AIDS |
| **Dosage** | 400–800mg/day (oral suspension more commonly used, but tablets applicable) |
| **Response** | Significant appetite improvement and weight gain |
| **Duration** | 12 weeks minimum assessment; long-term use as needed |
| **Evidence** | Multiple RCTs demonstrate significant weight gain vs placebo |

**Clinical Pearls:**
- FDA-approved indication (oral suspension formulation)
- Weight gain is predominantly fat
- Monitor for adrenal suppression with long-term use
- Check drug interactions with antiretrovirals (CYP3A4)
- Not a substitute for antiretroviral therapy

---

### B. OFF-LABEL / EVIDENCE-SUPPORTED INDICATIONS

---

### **5. Ovarian Cancer (Advanced)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Setting** | Recurrent/refractory ovarian cancer, palliative |
| **Dosage** | 160–320mg/day |
| **Evidence Level** | Phase II trials; limited evidence |
| **Response Rate** | 10–20% in hormone receptor-positive tumors |
| **Use** | When standard chemotherapy options exhausted |

---

### **6. Prostate Cancer (Advanced)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Setting** | Castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) |
| **Dosage** | 160mg TID–QID (480–640mg/day) |
| **Mechanism** | Antigonadotropic effect; direct antitumor activity |
| **Evidence** | Case series; limited modern evidence |
| **Use** | Historical use; largely replaced by newer agents (enzalutamide, abiraterone) |

---

### **7. Endometrial Hyperplasia (Complex Atypical)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Setting** | Fertility-sparing treatment; patients unfit for surgery |
| **Dosage** | 160–320mg/day |
| **Duration** | 3–6 months with endometrial biopsy monitoring |
| **Response Rate** | 50–80% regression in atypical hyperplasia |
| **Monitoring** | Serial endometrial biopsies every 3 months |

---

### **8. Hot Flashes (Severe/Refractory)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Setting** | Postmenopausal women or men on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) |
| **Dosage** | 20–40mg/day (low dose); 160mg tablet can be split or lower strength used |
| **Efficacy** | Reduces hot flash frequency by 75–85% |
| **Evidence** | Multiple RCTs supporting efficacy |
| **Use** | When estrogen therapy is contraindicated |

---

### **9. Unexplained Weight Loss in Elderly / Geriatric Cachexia**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Setting** | Non-cancer elderly patients with unexplained weight loss, failure to thrive |
| **Dosage** | 160–400mg/day |
| **Duration** | Short-term (weeks); reassess risk-benefit |
| **Caution** | Higher risk of DVT, adrenal suppression in elderly |

---

### **10. Renal Cell Carcinoma (Advanced)**

| **Parameter** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Setting** | Historically used before era of targeted therapy |
| **Dosage** | 160mg QID |
| **Current Role** | Largely obsolete; replaced by TKIs and immunotherapy |

---

items.title: COMPREHENSIVE USE CASES (Clinical Scenarios)
items.content:
### **USE CASE 1: Postmenopausal Woman with Metastatic Breast Cancer**

**Scenario:** 68-year-old postmenopausal woman with ER+/PR+ metastatic breast cancer. Failed tamoxifen (5 years adjuvant) and letrozole (progressed after 18 months). Multiple bone metastases. ECOG PS 2.

| **Decision Point** | **Action** |
|---|---|
| Why MEGATite? | Third-line hormonal therapy; patient prefers non-chemo option |
| Dose | 160mg QID (640mg/day) |
| Duration | Minimum 8–12 weeks to assess |
| Monitoring | Clinical exam, tumor markers (CA 15-3), imaging q3 months |
| Expected Outcome | Stable disease or partial response in 20–30% |
| Added Benefit | Appetite stimulation (patient also has mild cachexia) |

---

### **USE CASE 2: Advanced Endometrial Cancer in Elderly Patient Unfit for Surgery**

**Scenario:** 82-year-old with Grade 1 endometrial adenocarcinoma (PR-positive). Multiple comorbidities (CHF, CKD). Not a surgical candidate. Symptomatic vaginal bleeding.

| **Decision Point** | **Action** |
|---|---|
| Why MEGATite? | Hormonal palliation in non-surgical candidate |
| Dose | 160mg BID–QID (320–640mg/day) |
| Monitoring | Repeat endometrial biopsy at 3 months; MRI for tumor assessment |
| Response | Bleeding reduction, tumor stabilization |
| Risk Assessment | Monitor for DVT (limited mobility); adrenal function |

---

### **USE CASE 3: Cancer Cachexia in Lung Cancer Patient**

**Scenario:** 55-year-old male with Stage IV NSCLC on palliative chemotherapy. Lost 15% body weight over 3 months. Poor appetite. Albumin 2.5 g/dL. ECOG PS 2-3.

| **Decision Point** | **Action** |
|---|---|
| Goal | Appetite stimulation, weight stabilization, QoL improvement |
| Dose | 160mg TID (480mg/day), can escalate to 800mg/day |
| Duration | 8–12 weeks initial assessment |
| Adjunctive Therapy | Nutritional counseling, oral supplements |
| Expected Outcome | Appetite improvement (60–80%), 2–5 kg weight gain |
| Limitations | Weight gain = fat (not lean mass); no survival benefit |
| Monitoring | Weight, appetite diary, thromboembolic risk, blood glucose |

---

### **USE CASE 4: HIV/AIDS Wasting Syndrome**

**Scenario:** 40-year-old HIV-positive male on ART. CD4 count 150. BMI 16.5. Lost 12 kg over 6 months. Chronic diarrhea managed. No opportunistic infections currently.

| **Decision Point** | **Action** |
|---|---|
| Goal | Reverse wasting, improve nutritional status |
| Dose | 160mg TID–QID (480–640mg/day) |
| Duration | 12 weeks minimum |
| Drug Interactions | Check ART interactions (ritonavir ↑ megestrol levels via CYP3A4) |
| Monitoring | Weight, BMI, adrenal function (cortisol), blood glucose, lipids |
| Additional | Optimize ART, treat GI infections, nutritional supplementation |

---

### **USE CASE 5: Fertility-Sparing Treatment of Endometrial Hyperplasia**

**Scenario:** 32-year-old nulliparous woman with complex atypical endometrial hyperplasia on D&C. Desires future fertility. BMI 38.

| **Decision Point** | **Action** |
|---|---|
| Goal | Achieve endometrial regression; preserve fertility |
| Dose | 160mg BID (320mg/day) |
| Duration | 3–6 months |
| Monitoring | Repeat endometrial biopsy at 3 months and 6 months |
| Success Criteria | Complete regression on biopsy → attempt conception |
| Adjunctive | Weight loss counseling, metformin consideration |
| Failure Plan | Hysterectomy if no regression by 6–9 months |

---

### **USE CASE 6: Hot Flashes in Prostate Cancer Patient on ADT**

**Scenario:** 72-year-old male on LHRH agonist (leuprolide) for prostate cancer. Severe hot flashes (15–20/day). Affecting sleep and quality of life. Failed venlafaxine.

| **Decision Point** | **Action** |
|---|---|
| Dose | Lower doses preferred (20–40mg/day); 160mg tablet may be excessive |
| Alternative | Consider lower-strength formulation if available |
| Efficacy | 75–85% reduction in hot flash frequency |
| Risk | Potential tumor-stimulating effect in prostate cancer (controversial) |
| Monitoring | PSA levels, thromboembolic risk |

---

items.title: CONTRAINDICATIONS
items.content:
### **ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS**

| **# ** | **Contraindication** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Known hypersensitivity** to megestrol acetate or any excipient | Risk of anaphylaxis, angioedema, severe allergic reaction |
| 2 | **Pregnancy (Category X)** | Teratogenic; causes feminization of male fetus, genital abnormalities |
| 3 | **Suspected or confirmed pregnancy** | Must rule out pregnancy before initiation |
| 4 | **Active thromboembolic disease** (DVT, PE, stroke) | Megestrol increases thrombotic risk significantly |
| 5 | **Undiagnosed vaginal bleeding** | Must exclude malignancy before hormonal therapy |
| 6 | **Severe hepatic impairment / Active liver disease** | Hepatic metabolism; risk of accumulation and hepatotoxicity |
| 7 | **Known or suspected hormone-dependent tumor** (where progestins may stimulate growth — e.g., meningioma) | Risk of tumor progression |
| 8 | **Concurrent use for diagnostic pregnancy testing** | Progestational effects interfere with results |
| 9 | **Breastfeeding** | Excreted in breast milk; risk to infant |
| 10 | **History of megestrol-induced adrenal crisis** | Risk of recurrent life-threatening adrenal insufficiency |

---

### **RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATIONS / USE WITH EXTREME CAUTION**

| **#** | **Condition** | **Concern** | **Management** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **History of thromboembolic events** (prior DVT/PE) | Increased recurrence risk | Risk-benefit analysis; consider prophylactic anticoagulation |
| 2 | **Diabetes mellitus** | Megestrol causes hyperglycemia, insulin resistance | Frequent glucose monitoring; adjust antidiabetics |
| 3 | **Cardiovascular disease** (CHF, CAD) | Fluid retention, edema, thrombosis risk | Close cardiac monitoring; low-dose diuretics |
| 4 | **Adrenal insufficiency / Chronic steroid use** | Megestrol suppresses HPA axis; adrenal crisis risk | Do NOT stop abruptly; taper; monitor cortisol |
| 5 | **Epilepsy** | Fluid retention may lower seizure threshold | Monitor seizure frequency |
| 6 | **Renal impairment** | Fluid retention; edema | Dose adjustment may be needed; monitor fluid status |
| 7 | **Migraine** | Hormonal modulation may worsen migraines | Monitor headache frequency |
| 8 | **Depression** | Progestins may exacerbate depressive symptoms | Screen regularly; psychiatric referral if worsening |
| 9 | **Obesity / Metabolic syndrome** | Further weight gain; worsening metabolic parameters | Monitor weight, glucose, lipids |
| 10 | **Elderly patients (>75 years)** | Higher risk of DVT, adrenal suppression, Cushingoid effects | Lowest effective dose; short duration |
| 11 | **Immobilized patients** | High DVT/PE risk | Consider VTE prophylaxis |
| 12 | **Concurrent anticoagulant therapy** | Interaction potential; megestrol may affect warfarin metabolism | Monitor INR closely |

---

items.title: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS
items.content:
### **BLACK BOX / CRITICAL WARNINGS**

| **Warning** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Thromboembolic Events** | Increased risk of DVT, PE, thrombophlebitis, cerebrovascular events. Risk especially elevated in cancer patients, immobilized patients, and those with prior history |
| **Adrenal Suppression** | Chronic use suppresses HPA axis → adrenal insufficiency. **DO NOT stop abruptly.** Taper gradually. Patients may need stress-dose steroids during illness/surgery |
| **New-Onset Diabetes / Hyperglycemia** | Can precipitate diabetes or worsen glycemic control. Monitor fasting glucose regularly |
| **Cushing Syndrome (Iatrogenic)** | Moon face, buffalo hump, central obesity, striae with prolonged use |
| **Pregnancy Category X** | Absolutely contraindicated. Ensure reliable contraception in reproductive-age women |

---

### **OTHER IMPORTANT PRECAUTIONS**

| **Area** | **Precaution** |
|---|---|
| **Vaginal Bleeding** | Breakthrough bleeding or amenorrhea may occur |
| **Tumor Flare** | Possible initial flare in hormone-sensitive cancers (bone pain, hypercalcemia) |
| **Immunosuppression** | Mild glucocorticoid-like immunosuppressive effect with high doses |
| **Pneumonia Risk** | Some studies suggest increased pneumonia risk in elderly |
| **Carpal Tunnel Syndrome** | Rare; due to fluid retention |

---

items.title: DOSING GUIDELINES
items.content:
### **Indication-Based Dosing Summary**

| **Indication** | **Dose** | **Frequency** | **Total Daily Dose** | **Duration** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Breast cancer (advanced) | 160mg | QID | 640mg/day | Until progression |
| Endometrial cancer (advanced) | 160mg | BID–QID | 320–640mg/day | Until progression |
| Cancer cachexia/anorexia | 160mg | BID–TID (up to QID) | 320–800mg/day | Weeks to months |
| HIV wasting | 160mg | TID–QID | 480–800mg/day | 12+ weeks |
| Endometrial hyperplasia | 160mg | BID | 320mg/day | 3–6 months |
| Hot flashes | Lower doses preferred (20–80mg) | OD–BID | 20–80mg/day | As needed |

### **Administration Notes:**
- Take with food (improves absorption by 50–100%)
- Can be taken with or without water
- No crushing needed (swallow whole)
- Store at room temperature (15–30°C)
- Consistent timing recommended

---

items.title: DRUG INTERACTIONS
items.content:
| **Interacting Drug** | **Effect** | **Clinical Significance** | **Management** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Warfarin** | May increase or decrease INR | Moderate | Monitor INR closely; adjust warfarin dose |
| **Insulin / Oral hypoglycemics** | Megestrol causes hyperglycemia | High | Monitor glucose; adjust antidiabetic doses |
| **CYP3A4 inhibitors** (ketoconazole, ritonavir, clarithromycin) | Increased megestrol levels | Moderate | Monitor for toxicity; consider dose reduction |
| **CYP3A4 inducers** (rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine) | Decreased megestrol levels | Moderate | May need dose increase; monitor efficacy |
| **Dofetilide** | Increased dofetilide levels | High | Avoid combination if possible |
| **Corticosteroids** | Additive adrenal suppression | High | Avoid concurrent use; monitor cortisol |
| **Indinavir** | Megestrol may decrease indinavir levels | Moderate | Monitor viral load; consider ART adjustment |
| **Aminoglutethimide** | Decreases megestrol plasma concentrations | Moderate | Avoid combination |
| **Vaccines (live)** | Potential reduced immunogenicity | Low-Moderate | Avoid live vaccines during treatment |

---

items.title: ADVERSE EFFECTS
items.content:
### **Common (>10%)**

| **System** | **Adverse Effects** |
|---|---|
| **Metabolic** | Weight gain (most common), increased appetite, hyperglycemia |
| **Endocrine** | Adrenal suppression, Cushingoid features, amenorrhea |
| **GI** | Nausea, flatulence, diarrhea, constipation |
| **Cardiovascular** | Edema, hypertension |
| **Neurological** | Headache, insomnia, mood changes |
| **Reproductive** | Vaginal bleeding/spotting, decreased libido |

### **Uncommon (1–10%)**

| **System** | **Adverse Effects** |
|---|---|
| **Cardiovascular** | Thrombophlebitis, DVT |
| **Dermatological** | Rash, alopecia, hirsutism |
| **Musculoskeletal** | Carpal tunnel syndrome |
| **Respiratory** | Dyspnea, pneumonia |
| **Psychiatric** | Depression, anxiety |

### **Rare but Serious (<1%)**

| **Adverse Effect** | **Details** |
|---|---|
| **Pulmonary embolism** | Can be fatal; monitor for dyspnea, chest pain |
| **Adrenal crisis** | On abrupt withdrawal; Addisonian crisis |
| **Cerebrovascular accident (stroke)** | Thrombotic etiology |
| **Tumor flare / Hypercalcemia** | Especially in breast cancer with bone metastases |
| **Glucose intolerance → DKA** | Rare; in susceptible patients |
| **Hepatotoxicity** | Cholestatic jaundice (rare) |

---

items.title: MONITORING PARAMETERS
items.content:
### **Baseline Assessments**

| **Test** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|
| Complete blood count | Baseline; monitor for anemia, infection |
| Comprehensive metabolic panel | Renal and hepatic function |
| Fasting blood glucose / HbA1c | Baseline glycemic status |
| Lipid panel | Metabolic baseline |
| Morning cortisol / ACTH | Baseline adrenal function |
| Coagulation profile (PT/INR) | If on anticoagulants |
| Pregnancy test (females) | Rule out pregnancy (Category X) |
| Body weight / BMI | Baseline for monitoring |
| Blood pressure | Baseline cardiovascular assessment |
| Tumor markers (as applicable) | CA 125, CA 15-3, PSA |
| Imaging (as applicable) | Baseline tumor assessment |

### **Ongoing Monitoring Schedule**

| **Parameter** | **Frequency** |
|---|---|
| Weight | Weekly × 4, then monthly |
| Blood glucose | Weekly × 4, then monthly |
| Blood pressure | Every visit |
| Signs of DVT/PE | Every visit; patient education |
| Hepatic function (LFTs) | Monthly × 3, then q3 months |
| Morning cortisol | Every 3 months (or if symptoms of adrenal insufficiency) |
| Tumor response assessment | Every 2–3 months (imaging + markers) |
| Cushingoid features | Every visit (clinical assessment) |
| Mood/Depression screening | Every visit |
| Edema assessment | Every visit |

---

items.title: WHEN TO DISCONTINUE
items.content:
| **Scenario** | **Action** |
|---|---|
| Disease progression confirmed | Discontinue; switch therapy |
| Severe thromboembolic event (DVT/PE) | Discontinue immediately |
| Adrenal crisis | Emergency management; gradual taper after stabilization |
| Uncontrolled hyperglycemia/new-onset diabetes | Consider dose reduction or discontinuation |
| Intolerable side effects | Gradual dose taper (do NOT stop abruptly) |
| Pregnancy confirmed | Discontinue immediately |
| No response after 2–3 months (cancer indication) | Consider alternative therapy |
| Severe hepatotoxicity | Discontinue; supportive care |

### **⚠️ CRITICAL: TAPERING PROTOCOL**
- **Never stop megestrol abruptly after >2–4 weeks of use**
- Taper over 1–2 weeks minimum
- Consider stress-dose hydrocortisone coverage during illness/surgery
- Monitor for adrenal insufficiency symptoms: fatigue, hypotension, hyponatremia, nausea

---

items.title: EVIDENCE SUMMARY
items.content:
| **Indication** | **Key Evidence** | **Level of Evidence** |
|---|---|---|
| Breast cancer | Multiple phase II/III trials; response rate 20–30% | **Level I-II** |
| Endometrial cancer | Phase II/III trials; GOG studies | **Level I-II** |
| Cancer cachexia | Meta-analyses (Cochrane); significant appetite/weight improvement | **Level I** |
| HIV wasting | FDA-approved; multiple RCTs | **Level I** |
| Endometrial hyperplasia | Retrospective/prospective cohorts | **Level III** |
| Hot flashes | RCTs (Loprinzi et al.) | **Level I-II** |
| Ovarian cancer | Phase II trials; limited | **Level III** |
| Prostate cancer | Case series; historical | **Level IV** |

---

items.title: RESIDENT COMPETENCY CHECKLIST
items.content:
### **Knowledge Assessment Points**

- [ ] Can explain mechanism of action (antineoplastic + appetite stimulant)
- [ ] Can list all approved indications
- [ ] Can list all off-label indications with supporting evidence
- [ ] Can identify absolute vs relative contraindications
- [ ] Can prescribe correct dose for each indication
- [ ] Can identify key drug interactions
- [ ] Can create an appropriate monitoring plan
- [ ] Can recognize and manage adrenal suppression
- [ ] Can counsel patients on thromboembolic risk
- [ ] Can perform appropriate dose tapering
- [ ] Can distinguish fat gain vs lean mass gain in cachexia patients
- [ ] Can identify when to discontinue therapy

### **Clinical Skills Assessment**

- [ ] Conduct pre-treatment assessment (labs, pregnancy test, risk factors)
- [ ] Provide comprehensive patient counseling
- [ ] Document informed consent for off-label use
- [ ] Recognize and manage adverse effects
- [ ] Coordinate multidisciplinary care (oncology, nutrition, palliative)
- [ ] Manage emergency situations (adrenal crisis, PE)

---

items.title: PATIENT COUNSELING POINTS
items.content:
### **Key Messages for Patients**

1. **Take with food** for better absorption
2. **Do not stop suddenly** — always taper under medical supervision
3. **Report immediately:** leg swelling/pain, chest pain, sudden breathlessness, severe headache
4. **Weight gain is expected** — primarily fat; discuss expectations
5. **Blood sugar monitoring** if diabetic or at risk
6. **Reliable contraception** is mandatory for women of reproductive age
7. **Keep all follow-up appointments** for blood tests and monitoring
8. **Carry a medical alert card** stating megestrol use (for adrenal insufficiency awareness)
9. **Inform all healthcare providers** (especially before surgery/dental procedures)
10. **Do not share medication** with others

---

items.title: REFERENCES & FURTHER READING
items.content:
1. Loprinzi CL, et al. "Megestrol acetate for the prevention of hot flashes." *NEJM*. 1994;331(6):347-352.
2. Ruiz Garcia V, et al. "Megestrol acetate for treatment of anorexia-cachexia syndrome." *Cochrane Database Syst Rev*. 2013.
3. Lentz SS, et al. "GOG study: High-dose megestrol in advanced endometrial carcinoma." *Gynecol Oncol*. 1996.
4. Von Roenn JH, et al. "Megestrol acetate in patients with AIDS-related cachexia." *Ann Intern Med*. 1994;121(6):393-399.
5. NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines — Breast Cancer, Endometrial Cancer, Palliative Care (latest edition)
6. Product prescribing information — Megestrol Acetate Tablets

---
